questions.question: Compare DAS, NAS, and SAN storage attachment methods with a table.
keyPoints: DAS: Direct connection (SATA/SAS), block-level, single host, highest performance, lowest cost
NAS: Network connection (LAN), file-level (NFS/CIFS), multiple clients, moderate performance
SAN: Dedicated storage network (FC/iSCSI), block-level, multiple servers, high performance, highest cost
Include comparison of scalability, management complexity, and best use cases

questions.question: Explain RAID levels 0, 1, and 5 with diagrams.
keyPoints: RAID 0: Striping, no redundancy, best performance, all data lost if one disk fails
RAID 1: Mirroring, duplicate data on two disks, 50% capacity overhead, tolerates 1 failure
RAID 5: Striping with distributed parity, (N-1) usable capacity, tolerates 1 failure
Draw block diagrams showing data and parity distribution

questions.question: Explain Network Attached Storage (NAS) with its architecture and protocols.
keyPoints: NAS is a specialized file server accessed over a standard network (LAN/WAN)
Provides file-level access to clients
Uses NFS protocol for Unix/Linux and CIFS/SMB for Windows
Advantages: easy file sharing, centralized management, scalable
Disadvantages: network bandwidth bottleneck, not suitable for high-performance block I/O

questions.question: What is a SAN? How does it differ from NAS?
keyPoints: SAN: Dedicated high-speed storage network connecting servers to shared storage
Provides block-level access (servers see SAN as local disk)
Uses Fibre Channel or iSCSI protocols
NAS provides file-level access, SAN provides block-level access
SAN is more expensive but better for databases and high-performance workloads

questions.question: Explain RAID levels 0, 1, 5, 6, and 10 with their advantages, disadvantages, minimum disks, and usable capacity.
keyPoints: RAID 0: Striping, min 2 disks, 100% capacity, no fault tolerance
RAID 1: Mirroring, min 2 disks, 50% capacity, tolerates 1 failure
RAID 5: Distributed parity, min 3 disks, (N-1)/N capacity, tolerates 1 failure
RAID 6: Double parity, min 4 disks, (N-2)/N capacity, tolerates 2 failures
RAID 10: Mirror+Stripe, min 4 disks, 50% capacity, tolerates 1 per pair
Include block distribution diagrams for each
